explicit_text: Eritrea is my homeland, located in eastern Africa, sharing borders with Sudan, Ethiopia, and Djibouti. It is a country with nine ethnic tribes, each with unique traditions, especially around coffee. I cherish the memories of celebrating Easter with my family there, where coffee brewed in a jebena played a central role in our gatherings. Even though I now live far away, Eritrea remains close to my heart through these traditions.

Even though I now live far away, this place remains close to my heart through these traditions.

explicit_text: Sudan is one of the neighboring countries to Eritrea, sharing a border with it. While I haven't spoken much about Sudan itself, its proximity to Eritrea is part of the regional context I grew up in. The coffee culture we have in Eritrea, using the jebena, is also popular in surrounding countries like Sudan. This connection reminds me of the broader cultural ties in this part of Africa.

explicit_text: Ethiopia borders Eritrea and shares some cultural similarities, especially when it comes to coffee traditions. The jebena coffee maker is popular in both Eritrea and Ethiopia, and the way coffee is brewed and served is a cherished ritual in both countries. Growing up, I was aware of these shared customs, which highlight the close ties between our peoples despite being different nations.

explicit_text: Djibouti is another country that borders Eritrea, located in the Horn of Africa. Though I haven't detailed much about Djibouti, it is part of the geographic and cultural landscape surrounding my home country. The coffee culture that I treasure in Eritrea, especially using the jebena, is also appreciated in neighboring countries like Djibouti, which connects us in subtle ways.

explicit_text: The jebena is a special clay coffee maker from Eritrea that holds a special place in my heart. It has a round base, a cylindrical neck, and a curved handle, and comes in many colors and designs like Selam, Eritrea, Asmara, and Peace. My family and I used the jebena during Easter celebrations and Sunday gatherings back home, brewing coffee in three rounds called awel, calatey, and bereka. Even after moving to Minnesota four years ago, we brought our jebena with us to keep our coffee traditions alive and remind us of home.

explicit_text: Asmara is the capital city of Eritrea, and it is also a name used for one of the jebena designs. When I see the jebena with the Asmara design, it brings back memories of my life in Eritrea and the times my family spent together there. Asmara represents not just a place but a connection to my roots and the cultural heritage we carry with us, even here in Minnesota.

explicit_text: I moved to Minnesota about four years ago with my family, bringing our jebena all the way from Eritrea. Here in Minnesota, we continue our tradition of making coffee using the jebena on Sundays after church and during holidays. Though we eat American food, the coffee ritual helps us remember our homeland and create new happy memories together. Minnesota has become our new home, but Eritrea and its traditions remain close to us.

Though we eat American food, the coffee ritual helps us remember where we come from and create new happy memories together. explicit_text: Easter was always a special time for my family back in Eritrea, when everyone would come to my house and stay for three days. During Easter, my mom would make coffee using the jebena, and we would all gather around, sharing stories, laughter, and snacks like bread, popcorn, and candy. These celebrations created warm memories that I cherish deeply, and even now, I try to keep that spirit alive with my family here in Minnesota.
